coredata.dc:description: Heteroatom doping is an efficient approach for increasing fluorescence efficiency of carbon dots (CDs). Co-doping with heteroatoms may add additional active sites to CDs and expand their different capabilities. A simple and low-cost hydrothermal method was used to prepare nitrogen- and also nitrogen- and sulfur-rich CDs-decorated graphene oxide, NCDs-GO and N,SCDs-GO, respectively. The as-prepared hybrid CDs-GO samples are responsive to pH and temperature variations, implying pH and temperature-dependent fluorescence properties. The different properties of the CDs-GO hybrids were investigated using transmission electron microscopy and also energy-dispersive X-ray, Raman, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopies. The ternary mixture of N,SCDs-GO, poly(2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate) (PDMAEMA), and rhodamine B (RB) was also used to detect pH changes via the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process, where N,SCDs-GO and RB play the role of donor and acceptor, respectively, in the PDMAEMA aqueous solution medium. Fluorescence and ultraviolet–visible spectroscopies were used to assess the feasibility and efficiency of the FRET process. The donor–acceptor ratio was changed to get the broadly adjustable photoluminescence emission from blue to red, and especially white light. The aqueous ternary mixture has a wide range of potential applications in pH sensing, temperature monitoring, and anticounterfeiting. This colloidal system with controllable fluorescence and white light emission was used to design anticounterfeiting inks for security-marking of wood samples.

head.ce:title: Temperature- and pH-modulated Förster resonance energy transfer for establishment of white light emission

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heteroatom doping is an efficient approach for increasing fluorescence efficiency of carbon dots (CDs). Co-doping with heteroatoms may add additional active sites to CDs and expand their different capabilities. A simple and low-cost hydrothermal method was used to prepare nitrogen- and also nitrogen- and sulfur-rich CDs-decorated graphene oxide, NCDs-GO and N,SCDs-GO, respectively. The as-prepared hybrid CDs-GO samples are responsive to pH and temperature variations, implying pH and temperature-dependent fluorescence properties. The different properties of the CDs-GO hybrids were investigated using transmission electron microscopy and also energy-dispersive X-ray, Raman, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopies. The ternary mixture of N,SCDs-GO, poly(2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate) (PDMAEMA), and rhodamine B (RB) was also used to detect pH changes via the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process, where N,SCDs-GO and RB play the role of donor and acceptor, respectively, in the PDMAEMA aqueous solution medium. Fluorescence and ultraviolet–visible spectroscopies were used to assess the feasibility and efficiency of the FRET process. The donor–acceptor ratio was changed to get the broadly adjustable photoluminescence emission from blue to red, and especially white light. The aqueous ternary mixture has a wide range of potential applications in pH sensing, temperature monitoring, and anticounterfeiting. This colloidal system with controllable fluorescence and white light emission was used to design anticounterfeiting inks for security-marking of wood samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon structures with a diameter of less than 10 nm and also have outstanding photoluminescence (PL) characteristics [1,2]. CDs are cost-effective, biocompatible, photostable, and minimally toxic; therefore, they have garnered significant interest in optoelectronics, bioimaging, and photocatalysis [3–6]. The hydrothermal approach is widely used for the synthesis of CDs due to its low-cost, simplicity, and eco-friendliness. However, this technique is time-consuming and necessitates high temperatures [7,8]. The majority of CDs exhibit a singular blue fluorescence when exposed to UV light irradiation, hence demonstrating little diversity and adjustability in their luminescence characteristics [9,10]. The introduction of heteroatoms, particularly nitrogen and/or sulfur, into a carbon structure is an innovative approach to improve performance of carbon surfaces [11] and most importantly modulate their optical properties [12–15], which significantly affect the band gap and electrical density leading to an enhancement in their quantum yield (QY) [16]. Meanwhile, heteroatom-doped graphene oxide has been reported to exhibit remarkable photoluminescent properties [17,18]. Due to the unavoidable accumulation of graphene layers and low concentrations of the doped atoms, these methods possess evident drawbacks, such as reduced efficiency, presence of residual metallic impurities, and the need of using numerous organic precursors [19]. Therefore, it is essential to find a way to make a green, highly efficient, and environmentally acceptable technique of generating heteroatom-doped carbon-based materials with potent fluorescence properties [20,21].

CDs and their heteroatom-doped derivatives are highly applicable in smart polymers. There has been a significant interest in intelligent and functional materials that exhibit responsiveness to various environmental stimuli, such as temperature, pH, and CO2 [22–26]. Temperature and pH, in particular, are crucial indicators in many biological, chemical, and food-related processes [27–33]. A number of unique temperature sensors based on heteroatom-doped CDs have emerged. Guo et al. reported highly-efficient nitrogen- and also nitrogen- and sulfur-doped CDs (NCDs and N,SCDs, respectively) for use in temperature sensing [34]. Du et al. also reported application of NCDs and phosphorous-doped CDs (PCDs) in temperature sensors with high reversibility [35]. Liu et al. have developed a pH-sensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) and CDs for pH sensing [36]. FITC as the pH-sensitive fluorescent dye was covalently attached to the surface of CDs to make the FRET-based pH sensor, in which CDs act as donors and FITC acts as the acceptor and pH recognition moieties.

FRET is a process that involves the transfer of excited-state energy from an initially excited donor to an acceptor within a short distance between the dyads [37–39]. For an efficient energy transfer, the donor species should have a high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), the acceptor species should be in close proximity to the donor species, and there must be a good spectral overlap between the emission of the donor and the absorption of the acceptor. Selecting a good donor–acceptor combination is critical for attaining a white light emission [40]. The FRET-assisted white light emission has been known as an advanced anticounterfeiting technique because of its multilevel authentication system. In addition, the emission of CDs can be regulated by heteroatom doping and also using different exciting wavelengths that makes them as an effective anticounterfeiting materials [41,42]. Also, different fluorescent materials can be used as the FRET pairs of CDs, which are rhodamine [43], spiropyran [44], MnO2 [45], MoS2 [46], Au [47], and Ag [48] nanoparticles.

We demonstrate the fabrication of NCDs and N,SCDs decorated on graphene oxide (GO). Two doping agents of 1,3-propanediamine and thiourea were employed to investigate the effect of dopant type on the optical and sensing capabilities of the hybrid materials. The CDs-GO hybrid showed a great promise as a novel luminescent thermometer and pH sensor due to the strong temperature-dependence and pH-responsivity of the fluorescence emission spectra attributable to temperature-induced and pH-induced aggregation of CDs, respectively, resulting in an obvious fluorescence quenching. A FRET-based white light emission from N,SCDs-GO/poly(2- (dimethylamino)ethyl Methacrylate) (PDMAEMA) as the donor and rhodamine B (RB) as the acceptor has been developed. This research on FRET-based generation of white light emission with applications in optical sensors and smart inks will open a new window for the creation of novel light conversion materials with fascinating optical properties, high photostability, and low cytotoxicity. The as-prepared FRET-based anticounterfeiting ink is developed for the coloring of wood materials and also has applications in various industries, such as those dealing with confidential documents, packaging, and wooden works of art.

Graphite, citric acid, thiourea, and all of the solvents were purchased from the Merck-Millipore company. CuBr (Sigma-Aldrich, 98 %) was treated with glacial acetic acid (Carlo Erba), filtered, rinsed with ethanol, dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C, and kept under N2 before its application. Rhodamine B (RB, ≥95 %), 1,3 propanediamine (99 %), ethyl 2-bromoisobutyrate (EBiB, 97 %), and quinin sulfate (90 %) were received from Sigma Aldrich. Distilled-deionized (DI) water was used in all the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PDMAEMA via atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP)
ce:section.ce:para: ATRP was used to synthesize PDMAEMA with EBiB as the initiator using a recipe reported in literature [49]. CuBr (0.06 g, 0. 45 mmol) and THF (3 mL) were added to a Schlenk tube. The system was nitrogen-purged and deoxygenated using three freeze–pump–thaw cycles. The copper complex was then formed by adding PMDETA (0.094 mL, 0. 45 mmol) and stirring for 30 min. EBiB (0.066 mL, 0.45 mmol) was added to the mixture after adding PDMAEMA (1.88 g, 11.97 mmol) using a syringe. Under a N2 atmosphere, polymerization was permitted to proceed at 70 °C for 24 h. Copper residues were removed from the reaction product by passing it through a column of basic alumina and its purifying by precipitation with cool hexane. The homopolymer was subsequently filtered and vacuum-dried at 40 °C for 24 h.

GO was produced from graphite using the improved Hummers’ method [50]. The following hydrothermal approach was also used to prepare the NCDs-GO hybrid, Fig. 1
[51]. First, 20 mg of GO was mixed with 20 mL of DI water and thoroughly dispersed in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min. The obtained homogenous dispersion was placed in an autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 14 h. After reaching the room temperature, 340 mL of 1,3-propylenediamine was poured into the GO dispersion, which was then heated in an autoclave at 120 ℃ for an additional 3 h. After cooling, 0.9 g of citric acid dissolved in 3 mL of water was poured to the reaction mixture and heated at 180 ℃ for 12 h. The autoclave was cooled to 25 ℃ after the process. The final dispersion was centrifuged at 15000 rpm and the supernatant containing NCDs-GO was transferred to a dialysis bag (1000D) and dialyzed over 24 h.

The preparation approach for N,SCDs is briefly described below, Fig. 1. First, 20 mg of GO was mixed in 1 mg/mL of DI water, followed by 1 h of ultrasonic agitation. An autoclave was used to heat the resultant homogenous dispersion at 200 ℃ for 14 h. After reaching the ambient temperature, 0.33 g of citric acid and 0.44 g of thiourea (each dissolved in 3 mL of DI water) were added to the GO dispersion and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. Once the reaction is over, the autoclave was let to cool to room temperature. The resultant dispersion was centrifuged at 15000 rpm over 10 min before being transferred to a dialysis bag (1000 D) and dialyzed for 24 h.

Liquid–Liquid phase extraction was used to separate NCDs-GO and N,SCDs-GO from unreacted precursors or unwanted intermediates. For this purpose, 10 mL of chloroform was added to the primary dispersion, resulting in the formation of two immiscible phases. A coagulated product, formed by salting-out and flotation phenomena, flowed between two organic and aqueous phases in the funnel after the addition of a 30 % NaCl solution. The middle phase consisting of the NCDs or N,SCDs was isolated on graphene sheets, has now been separated from the two remaining phases. The separated product was then rinsed several times with DI water and centrifuged to eliminate any remaining salt and water-soluble contaminants. The final product was achieved after drying in an oven at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CDs-decorated GO samples
ce:section.ce:para: The thiourea molecules may create a seven-membered ring with another citric acid molecules. Finally, N,SCDs were used to functionalize the surface of GO layers to obtain N,SCDs-GO. Heteroatom doping has been shown to be an efficient approach of increasing the fluorescence efficiency of CDs (Fig. 1).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of GO shows a thin layer with a rough surface that was not crumpled in Fig. 2 a. The disordered structure was caused a high degree of exfoliation during the oxidation and oxidation processes [52]. TEM images were also used to assess the size, shape, and structure of the NCDs-GO and N,SCDs-GO hybrids. The presence of consistently scattered spherical CDs on the modified GO layers demonstrated the successful synthesis of NCDs-GO and N,SCDs-GO hybrids in Fig. 2b, c. To study the incorporation of N and S atoms in the structure of the NCDs-GO and N,SCDs-GO hybrids, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy FTIR was used, Fig. 2d. The stretching vibration of O–H is linked to a broad peak in the FTIR spectra of GO at 3417 cm–1. The bending vibrations of C=O are attributed to the peaks at 1724 and 1618 cm−1, whereas the stretching vibrational absorption of C–OH and C–O are assigned to the peaks at 1220 and 1049 cm−1. These characteristic absorption bands imply that GO's surface is rich in hydroxyl, epoxy, and carboxyl groups. In the case of NCDs-GO, a sharp peak at 3430 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H, while the minor peaks at 1626 and 1443 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C=O and C–OH, and the bending vibrations of C–N. This is due to the fact that several oxygen-containing groups on the surface of GO were replaced by –NH2 during the one-pot hydrothermal process. The stretching vibrations of hydroxyl and amine groups may be assigned to the band at 3434 cm−1. In the case of N,SCDs-GO, C=O and C=C vibrational peaks are found at 1700 and 1564 cm−1, respectively. Furthermore, the peaks at 1189 cm−1 belong to C–N stretching vibrations, whereas the peaks at 609 and 870 cm−1 correspond to C–S stretching vibrations. The FT-IR spectra show that simultaneous reduction of GO and doping of CDs with their incorporation at the surface of GO were successfully carried out. Raman spectroscopy is also used to show the structural defects of carbon-based materials in the most direct and nondestructive way. The number of defects on graphene materials may be determined by calculating the ratio of the peak intensities of Raman spectra for the breathing mode of carbon atoms with sp3 hybridization (D) and graphitic carbon with sp2 hybridization (G) bands, which is written as ID/IG. Raman spectra of the pure GO, NCDs-GO, and N,SCDs-GO are shown in Fig. 2e. It is evident that the ID/IG amount is lower for GO (0.825) compared with NCDs-GO (0.828) and N,SCDs-GO (0.834). This suggests that N,SCDs-GO have a lower crystallinity than NCDs-GO and GO due to its higher edge irregularity and higher defect content.

Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was performed on the NCDs-GO and N,SCDs-GO samples using elemental mapping to confirm the presence of C, N, O, and S atoms in their structure. Various elements, including C (63.2 %), N (15.7 %), and O (20.9 %), can be seen in the EDX spectrum of the NCDs-GO hybrid sample, Fig. S1(a) (Supporting Information). The EDX spectrum of the N,SCDs-GO hybrid (Fig. S1(b)) (Supporting Information) shows that it is composed of N (12.2 %), S (7.5 %), C (62.5 %), and O (15.1 %) elements. The elemental mapping results for nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) exhibit similarities with the patterns observed in the selected area of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. This suggests that both sulfur and nitrogen are evenly distributed within the N-CDs/GO and N,S-CDs/GO samples.

Fig. 3 a shows that NCDs-GO displays two UV–vis absorption peaks at 265 and 348 nm in an aqueous media, whereas the citric acid absorption is below 230 nm. The narrow peak at 265 nm, which corresponds to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, results in almost no observable PL signal. The other transition with a significant emission at 348 nm is observed owing to the capture of excited state energy by the surface state [53,54]. N,SCD-GO has a more pronounced absorption band at 226 nm, which is in accordance with the π-π* transition of the C=C bond [55], as well as the shoulder at 341 nm as a possible indication of n- π* transitions in C=O. The differences in absorption spectra between the two kinds of CDs are most likely due to the presence of S dopants in N,SCDs-GO, which represent the interaction of the dopants with the primary absorbing species on the sp2 platform. The strong peak at 232 nm is generated by the π-π* transition of the electrons of C=C present in the CDs after mixing with PDMAEMA, while the two faint peaks at 276 nm and 340 nm are produced by the n-π* transition of the C=O bond. Fig. 3b demonstrates that N,SCDs-GO has higher PL emission intensity than NCDs-GO. Utilizing quinine sulfate as a standard, the PLQY of N,SCDs-GO was determined to be 32 % in an aqueous medium; this efficiency was more than that of the N, CDs-GO's highest fluorescence QY of 27 %. Emissive traps [56,57] and electronic conjugate structures [58] are two prevalent possibilities for the PL mechanism of CDs, although the exact process is still unclear. The absorption at 226 nm attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains of the N,S-CDs-GO produces almost no observed PL signal, indicating that the PL of N,S-CDs-GO should not be generated through the electronic conjugated structures The surface states of CDs ought to have a large effect on their photophysical properties [59]. The PL of CDs can be attributed to the radiative recombination of electrons and holes confined on the CDs' surface. Therefore, the intense PL emission of the N,SCDs-GO in comparison to the NCDs-GO is primarily due to the surface-doped N and/or S atoms. The optical bandgaps of NCDs-GO and N,SCDs-GO were calculated using
( α h ν )

A ( h ν -
E g
), where α is the extinction coefficient, h is Planck’s constant, υ is the frequency of vibration light, A is the proportional constant, and E g is the bandgap. The value of r is 1/2 for an indirect band gap material and 2 for a direct band gap material. Fig. 3c and d demonstrate a linear correlation when using r = 2, suggesting that the as-produced NCDs-GO and N,SCDs-GO possess direct band gap characteristics. Conversely, a satisfactory linear fit is not achieved for r = 1/2. The values of the energy band gap obtained for NCDs-GO and N,SCDs-GO are 5.26 and 5.19 eV, respectively. The large band gap in NCDs-GO compared to N,SCDs-GO could be due to the strong negative induction effect of the N atom in NCDs-GO, which drastically reduces the π electron cloud of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the temperature sensing ability
ce:section.ce:para: The temperature-dependent PL spectra of NCDs-GO, N,SCDs-GO, and N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA aqueous dispersions were studied at different temperatures ranging from 25 to 85 °C at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm, Fig. 4 . The fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing temperature and recovered when the temperature turned back to 25 ℃, which can be ascribed to the aggregation of CDs causing the evident suppression of fluorescence emission. The CDs showed remarkable reversibility and restoration by varying the temperature [60]. The relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) of the samples as a function of temperature was studied to show the effect of temperature variation on fluorescence intensity of the samples, where I0 is the fluorescence intensity at 25 ℃ [61]. The R2 values of linear regression fitting for NCDs-GO, N,SCDs-GO, and N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA are 0.9766, 0.971, and 0.9676, respectively, indicating that all the three thermometers are very accurate. The thermometer's reversibility was further analyzed by monitoring changes in fluorescence intensity when samples were cooled from 85 to 25 ℃, Fig. S2a–c (Supporting Information). For N CDs-GO, N,SCDs-GO, and N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA, the increase of I/I0 with decreasing temperature throughout the cooling progression exhibits a strong linear relationship, with corresponding R2 values of 0.9799, 0.948, and 0.9695, Fig. S3a–c (Supporting Information). The linear change of I/I0 with temperature exhibits the ability of the CDs-decorated GO and also N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA samples as a precise thermometer to measure the temperature by varying fluorescence emission in the temperature range of 25 to 85 ℃.

Temperature sensors play a crucial role in determination of the temperature resulting from exothermic processes or monitoring the temperature of media. To determine the temperature of an unidentified sample, the calibration curve derived from PL spectroscopy of the N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA sample was selected. To determine the approximate temperature of the unknown sample, a PL analysis was performed, Fig. 5 a. Then, by linear fitting of I/I0 data at 7 temperatures, the temperature of the unknown sample was estimated, as shown in Fig. 5b. The calibration curve derived from fluorescence spectroscopy of the N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA sample was obtained using the identical route and afterwards used to estimate the virtual temperature of the unknown sample. The disparity between the measured temperature of the unidentified sample and the temperature estimated from the curves exhibited a discrepancy of less than 0.5 °C for both the data obtained from fluorescence spectroscopy and the thermometer.PDMAEMA is a polycationic polymer characterized by the presence of tertiary amine groups. It exhibits modest polybasic properties and displays intriguing solubility behavior depending on temperature. It is known as a thermos-responsive polymer with a lower critical solution temperature (LCST) due to the breakdown of hydrogen bonds upon the solid polymer's separation from the solution [62–64]. The LCST of PDMAEMA in an aqueous solution is shown in a temperature range of 45 to 50 °C, which is affected by polymer composition, molecular weight, ionic strength, and concentration of solution [65] Fig. 4c shows the PL spectra of the N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA hybrids at various temperatures ranging from 25 to 85 °C. The PL intensity of the hybrids decreased with increasing temperature. In addition, a significant reduction in intensity was observed at around 54 °C, which is due to the wrapping of the fluorescent units on the graphene layers by the PDMAEMA chains at above their LCST.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the pH-dependent optical properties
ce:section.ce:para: The PL spectra of NCDs-GO, N,SCDs-GO, and N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA at different pH values (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11) are shown in Fig. 6 . The optical examination of NCDs-GO reveals that the emission intensity increased significantly as the pH increases, reaching a maximum at a pH of 5. This tendency is accompanied with a noticeable blue shift in the maximum emission wavelength from 460 to 410 nm, Fig. 6a. The emission wavelength remained almost constant throughout the pH range of 5 to 11; however, the emission intensity varied to some extent. Increasing the pH further resulted in a considerable decrease in emission intensity. As presented in Fig. 6b and c, the PL intensity of N,SCD-GO and N,SCD-GO/PDMAEMA increases largely when the pH increased from 1 to 7, and remained stable at pH values of 9 and 11. Consequently, as the pH of the media changes from neutral to acidic, they aggregate into large particles and get blue-shifted to a higher wavelength that known as an inverse PL shift [66]. Furthermore, at acidic pH values, nitrogen atoms are progressively protonated, and proton transfer to the conjugated carbon structure increases fluorescence intensity. The deprotonation of functional groups at higher pH values substantially generates new surface states with a smaller band gap at CDs as the media approaches alkaline conditions; these states are liable for the absorption and emission of CDs at alkaline pHs. These observations reveal a unified picture for the PL of CDs that demonstrates a synergistic effect between the surface states and the carbogenic interior of CDs. Resulting from this interaction, the PL of CDs can be modified through straightforward chemical modifications [67]. When N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA is compared to N,SCDs-GO and NCDs-GO, there is a considerable increase in fluorescence intensity at pH 1 to 7. At pH levels lower than the pKa of PDMAEMA (7.4), the homopolymer chains were stretched due to the protonation of the tertiary amine groups of PDMAEMA, Fig. 6d.

The incorporation of heteroatoms through doping enables the adjustment of the PL emission spectral position in CDs [68,69] and the optimization of spectral overlap with acceptor molecules [70]. By doping N and S atoms, the position of the PL emission center and the PLQY value of CDs were modified. As a result, as an emerging donor species, N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA are gained to create the FRET phenomenon in the presence of RB as the acceptor. Fig. 7 b shows the PL emission spectra of the N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA@RB system as well as the corresponding spectra of N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA and RB at similar concentrations to those found in the system. In addition, the chosen excitation wavelength to collect the PL emission is located in the part of absorption minimum of RB as an acceptor molecule (Fig. 7a). This fact assures excitation of N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA@RB by emission of N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA. The PL spectrum of N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA@RB shows one band at 440 nm for N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA and another at 580 nm for RB, as shown in Fig. 7b. The N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA@RB system exhibits an undeniable decrease in the PL emission band intensity of N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA and an increase in the PL emission band intensity of RB. These observations rule out the possibility of RB emission enhancement via direct excitation and provide an indication of the FRET mechanism. Molecular structures of the fluorescent N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA and RB and the FRET mechanism between them are exhibited in Fig. 7c.

The FRET phenomenon was obviously observed upon excitation of the RB-doped N,SCDs/GO aqueous dispersion at λex = 340 nm. At a constant pH of 7, the FRET efficiency was investigated by increasing the concentration of RB from 0 to 20 μL, and 20 μL was determined to be the optimal concentration of RB for the highest FRET efficiency, Fig. 8 a. Light emission with different values of CIE coordinates is attained in the N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA@RB system by varying the concentration of RB, Fig. 8b. At a certain concentration of RB (20 μL), N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA@RB represents WLE with PLQY and CIE coordinate of 65 % and (0.3, 0.33), respectively. Fig. 8c displays the digital photographs of emission colors of N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA@RB with various concentration of RB.

The pH sensing mechanism of the N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA@RB aqueous dispersions was also studied. Fig. 9 depicts the fluorescence spectra of N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA@RB aqueous dispersion at different pH values when stimulated at 340 nm. The emission spectra were found to span the whole visible range from 400 to 700 nm. RB may occur in two states of the open/fluorescent spirolactone and the closed/nonfluorescent spirolactone. In acidic conditions (pH of lower than 5), the open form predominates, whereas the closed form predominates in the basic conditions. Ring-opening kinetics are affected by solvent properties, which include solvent acidity and polarity. Acidic solvents promote the development of the open form of RB, but solvent polarity has a more different influence [71]. The fluorescence intensity at 580 nm enhances as the pH of the aqueous dispersion decreases, since RB exists in its more fluorescent dianion form at lower pH values. At reduced pH values, a substantial proportion of energy from the emission of N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA is transferred to RB. Due to a more efficient FRET, the fluorescence intensity of N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA decreases in this phenomenon. The FRET efficiency (E) is calculated to be 51 % using equation (1), where FDA is the fluorescence emission intensity of the donor (N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA) in the presence of the acceptor (RB) and FD is the fluorescence emission intensity of the acceptor.
(1)
E =
F
DA
/
F D

Fig. 10 a, b shows the images of the aqueous dispersions of N,SCDs-GO, N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA@RB, and RB before and after UV excitation, respectively. The blue and orange emissions of N,SCDs-GO and RB are obvious in the image. The color shown in the aqueous dispersion of N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA@RB approves the FRET phenomenon in the system. Foremost, the FRET-based aqueous ink was utilized for security marking of a wooden sample. The wooden sample under daylight and UV irradiation is shown in Fig. 10c, d, respectively. The white light emission of the U letter can easily be observed using the N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA@RB system.

The PL emission properties of N,SCDs-GO and N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA precisely tailored by decorating GO with N and N,S-doped CDs using a simple and low-cost hydrothermal process, as well as physically doping PDMAEMA. Highly luminescent NCDs-GO and N,SCDs-GO were synthesized by using (i) GO and citric acid, (ii) 1,3-propandiamine, and (iii) thiourea as carbon, nitrogen, and both of nitrogen and sulfur sources, respectively. PLQY of N,SCDs-GO was determined to be 32 % that showed a significant increase compared to that of for N, CDs-GO (27 %). The temperature dependence of the fluorescence emission of both the CDs-decorated GO and N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA was analyzed from 25 to 85 °C. Increasing temperatures dampen the fluorescence intensity, which was reversible and allowed the fluorescence to regain its initial intensity as the temperature decreases. As a result, the temperature influence on fluorescence intensity might be used to create a temperature sensor with high reversibility and sensitivity. A FRET-based white light emitting system, N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA@RB, with an energy transfer efficiency of 51 % was created. Doping an aqueous solution of RB (acceptor) into the aqueous dispersion of N,SCDs-GO/PDMAEMA (donor) resulted in the transfer of the donor's excited state to the acceptor and activation of the FRET mechanism for white light emission. The FRET efficiency was high when the amount of RB was 20 μL. The white light generating system's PLQY and CIE color coordinates are determined to be as high as 65 % and (0.3, 0.33), respectively. In an aqueous dispersion, the pH sensing capabilities of the FRET pairs were effectively demonstrated. As the pH of the aqueous medium decreases, the fluorescence intensity at λ = 580 nm increases, because RB exists in its more fluorescent dianion form at lower pH values. Most notably, the aqueous dispersion was utilized as an anticounterfeiting ink for security documents as well as the security of a wood handicrafts.
